blocks.text: And now, O priests, this command is for you.
1 : echoes language found in Deuteronomy (e.g., 27.1; 30.11).

If you will not listen, if you will not lay it to heart to give glory to my name, says the Lord of hosts, then I will send the curse on you and I will curse your blessings; indeed I have already cursed them, because you do not lay it to heart.
2 : Veneration of the 's is also at issue in Mal 1.6,11,14; 2.5. The diction of cursing continues that of 1.14. probably refers to the blessings that priests give (Num 6.23–27).
I will rebuke your offspring, and spread dung on your faces, the dung of your offerings, and I will put you out of my presence.
2.1–3 : Admonition and threat to the priests.

3 : implies the priestly lineage of Aaron, who were promised a covenant of peace (Num 25.12–13). , either excrement or intestinal contents normally burned outside the Temple compound (Ex 29.14; Lev 4.11; 8.17; 16.27; Num 19.5). Smearing on the face of a priest would require the removal of that unclean priest from the Temple.
Know, then, that I have sent this command to you, that my covenant with Levi may hold, says the Lord of hosts.
My covenant with him was a covenant of life and well-being, which I gave him; this called for reverence, and he revered me and stood in awe of my name.

4–5 : , the third son of Jacob and ancestor of all priests (Gen 29.34). Jer 33.21 refers to a ; Deut 33.9 affirms the covenantal fidelity of the Levites. or “peace” (Heb “shalom”), is associated with the covenant with Phinehas in Num 25.12.
True instruction was in his mouth, and no wrong was found on his lips. He walked with me in integrity and uprightness, and he turned many from iniquity.
6 : , Heb “torah.” Deut 33.10 characterizes Levites as responsible for teaching God's torah. To “walk” describes a proper covenantal relationship in Deuteronomy (10.12; 13.5).
For the lips of a priest should guard knowledge, and people should seek instruction from his mouth, for he is the messenger of the Lord of hosts.
7 : The language becomes prescriptive, though continuing to emphasize instruction. , a phrase used of Haggai (Hag 1.13; see also Gen 19.1; 21.17).
But you have turned aside from the way; you have caused many to stumble by your instruction; you have corrupted the covenant of Levi, says the Lord of hosts,
8 : probably refers to the priests, indicted earlier in this disputation. The metaphor of walking (v. 6) continues, though now in negative fashion: turning .
and so I make you despised and abased before all the people, inasmuch as you have not kept my ways but have shown partiality in your instruction.
1.6–2.9: Concerning priests and Levites. The longest of the disputations deals with the priesthood.
4–9 : The language of covenant and commandment continues, though now focusing on the Levites, whose status was below that of the priests who traced their ancestry back to Aaron.
9 : The punishment is consistent with 2.2–3. Neh 13.29–30 may provide a context for understanding these charges.
Have we not all one father? Has not one God created us? Why then are we faithless to one another, profaning the covenant of our ancestors?
10 : For a similar question, see Deut 32.6. On God as , see Deut 6.4. , cf. Deut 4.3–31; 29.24–25; Jer 34.13–14, all referring to the Sinai covenant.

Judah has been faithless, and abomination has been committed in Israel and in Jerusalem; for Judah has profaned the sanctuary of the Lord, which he loves, and has married the daughter of a foreign god.

11 : , a religious sin. , literally, “the holiness of Yahweh,” which may refer to the Temple (Pss 63.2; 68.22). may refer to veneration of a goddess such as Asherah or marriage to a non-Israelite woman, a major issue in the Persian period (see Ezra 9–10; Neh 13).
May the Lord cut off from the tents of Jacob anyone who does this—any to witness or answer, or to bring an offering to the Lord of hosts.
12 : The punishing curse. To as a punishment, see Pss 12.3; 109.15. , see Jer 30.18.
And this you do as well: You cover the Lord's altar with tears, with weeping and groaning because he no longer regards the offering or accepts it with favor at your hand.
13 : at the Temple may refer to foreign rituals similar to those described in Ezek 8.14.
You ask, “Why does he not?” Because the Lord was a witness between you and the wife of your youth, to whom you have been faithless, though she is your companion and your wife by covenant.
14 : The identity of the characters is ambiguous. If is Judah, then the would be the . On marriage as a covenant, see also Ezek 16.8; Prov 2.17.
Did not one God make her? Both flesh and spirit are his. And what does the one God desire? Godly offspring. So look to yourselves, and do not let anyone be faithless to the wife of his youth.
15 : Familial symbolism continues with reference to
For I hate divorce, says the Lord, the God of Israel, and covering one's garment with violence, says the Lord of hosts. So take heed to yourselves and do not be faithless.
2.10–16: Religious fidelity. Focus on covenant continues, but now between God and all Israel. Familial imagery remains important.

15b–16 : These verses, perhaps by another author, probably refer to human divorce; the issue is now marriage within the community, not idolatry as in vv. 10–14. Although Deut 24.1–4 permits , it is here likened to that the hates.

You have wearied the Lord with your words. Yet you say, “How have we wearied him?” By saying, “All who do evil are good in the sight of the Lord, and he delights in them.” Or by asking, “Where is the God of justice?”
2.17–3.5: Theodicy and theophany. The prospect of judgment brought by both a messenger and the .

17 : The people are quoted three times. The second quotation reverses the usual biblical view about God and evildoers (cf. Deut 18.12; 25.16). echoes language in psalms of lament, e.g., Pss 22.1; 89.49.
